perturbation.original_text: BROOKS, its directors, employees, agents and representatives shall not disclose, disseminate, publish, communicate or divulge any Confidential Information to anyone outside BROOKS, or to any employee of BROOKS not having reasonable need for access to such information, unless CLIENT expressly consents to such disclosure in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: BROOKS, its directors, employees, agents and representatives shall not disclose, disseminate, publish, communicate or divulge any Confidential Information to anyone outside BROOKS, or to any employee of BROOKS not having reasonable need for access to such information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'unless CLIENT expressly consents to such disclosure in writing' allows BROOKS to disclose confidential information without the client's consent, which may violate New York trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could be seen as deceptive business practices if the client is led to believe their information will be kept confidential yet there's no legal obligation for Brooks to not disclose it, contradicting NY GBL § 350 regarding false advertising and misleading business practices.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text stipulates that BROOKS shall not disclose confidential information unless the client consents in writing. The changed text removes this clause, allowing BROOKS to disclose confidential information without client consent. The Law Explanation states that this could be a deceptive business practice. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references NY GBL § 350 regarding false advertising and misleading business practices. Removing the clause gives BROOKS more freedom to use the information as it pleases, potentially contradicting deceptive business practices, as BROOKS no longer has a legal obligation to the client.

perturbation.original_text: If BROOKS is legally compelled (whether by regulatory request, deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigation, demand or similar process) to disclose any Confidential Information, BROOKS shall immediately notify the CLIENT in writing of such requirements so that the CLIENT may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions hereof. BROOKS will use its best efforts, at Macintosh HD:Users:vibrant:Downloads:CONFIDENTIALITY AGREEMENT.doc the CLIENT’S expense, to obtain or assist CLIENT in obtaining any such protective order. Failing the entry of a protective order to the receipt of a waiver hereunder, BROOKS may disclose, without liability hereunder, that portion (and only that portion) of Confidential Information that BROOKS has been advised by written opinion counsel reasonably acceptable to CLIENT that it is legally compelled to disclose; provided, however, that BROOKS agrees to use its best efforts to obtain assurance, at no cost to BROOKS, that confidential treatment will be accorded such Confidential Information by the person or persons to whom it is disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: If BROOKS is legally compelled (whether by regulatory request, deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigation, demand or similar process) to disclose any Confidential Information, BROOKS may disclose that portion (and only that portion) of Confidential Information that BROOKS has been advised by written opinion counsel that it is legally compelled to disclose.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for BROOKS to notify the CLIENT before disclosing confidential information under legal compulsion, and removing the 'best efforts' clause, potentially violates the client's right to protect their trade secrets under New York law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law & Rules § 3101
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the notification requirement, the clause contradicts the spirit of NY CPLR § 3101 regarding discovery, as the client loses the opportunity to seek a protective order to limit the scope of disclosure and protect their confidential information as allowed under the statute.

Scope of disclosure. (a) Generally. There shall be full disclosure of all matter material and necessary in the prosecution or defense of an action, regardless of the burden of proof, by: (1) a party, or the officer, director, member, agent or employee of a party; (2) a person who possessed a cause of action or defense asserted in the action; (3) a person about to depart from the state, or without the state, or residing at a greater distance from the place of trial than one hundred miles, or so sick or infirm as to afford reasonable grounds of belief that he or she will not be able to attend the trial, or a person authorized to practice medicine, dentistry or podiatry who has provided medical, dental or podiatric care or diagnosis to the party demanding disclosure, or who has been retained by such party as an expert witness; and (4) any other person, upon notice stating the circumstances or reasons such disclosure is sought or required. (b) Privileged matter. Upon objection by a person entitled to assert the privilege, privileged matter shall not be obtainable. (c) Attorney's work product. The work product of an attorney shall not be obtainable. (d) Trial preparation. 1. Experts. (i) Upon request, each party shall identify each person whom the party expects to call as an expert witness at trial and shall disclose in reasonable detail the subject matter on which each expert is expected to testify, the substance of the facts and opinions on which each expert is expected to testify, the qualifications of each expert witness and a summary of the grounds for each expert's opinion. However, where a party for good cause shown retains an expert an insufficient period of time before the commencement of trial to give appropriate notice thereof, the party shall not thereupon be precluded from introducing the expert's testimony at the trial solely on grounds of noncompliance with this paragraph.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates BROOKS to notify the CLIENT before disclosing confidential information under legal compulsion, allowing the CLIENT to seek a protective order. The changed text removes this notification requirement and the 'best efforts' clause. The Law Explanation states this contradicts NY CPLR § 3101, as the client loses the opportunity to seek a protective order. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NY CPLR § 3101, confirms the 'scope of disclosure,' and the right to defend an action, including actions to protect privileged matter under subsection (b). Removing the notification undermines the client's ability to protect confidential information, creating a legal contradiction as per the Omission Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed and interpreted by, and in accordance with, the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed and interpreted by, and in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware could disadvantage the client if New York law provides stronger protections for confidential information or trade secrets in this specific context. This could be considered an unfair or deceptive trade practice if the client is not aware of the difference in legal standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the governing law without proper disclosure and informed consent could constitute a deceptive act or practice under New York General Business Law § 349 if it disadvantages the client by reducing their rights or remedies compared to what they would have had under New York law, particularly concerning trade secrets.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies New York law, whereas the changed text switches it to Delaware law. The Law Explanation states this change could be a deceptive practice under New York General Business Law § 349 if it reduces the client's rights, particularly concerning trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Article 22-A of the New York General Business Law, concerning consumer protection from deceptive acts and practices, indicating a legal basis for consumer protection in New York. This change could disadvantage the client and therefore violates the law.
